cards.title: Definition
cards.body: Cyber security is the practice of protecting systems, networks, and programs from digital attacks. These attacks aim to access, change, or destroy sensitive information, extort money, or interrupt business processes.

cards.title: Why It Matters
cards.body: India reported over 14 lakh cyber incidents in 2023 alone. Personal data, bank accounts, and national infrastructure are all targets. Everyone — not just IT professionals — needs basic cyber hygiene.

cards.title: CIA Triad
cards.body:
The foundation of cyber security rests on three pillars:

• Confidentiality – only authorised users access data
• Integrity – data is accurate and untampered
• Availability – systems are accessible when needed

quiz.question: Which three principles form the foundation of cyber security?
quiz.explanation: The CIA Triad — Confidentiality, Integrity, and Availability — is the universally accepted model for evaluating information security policies.

cards.title: Malware
cards.body: Malware (malicious software) includes viruses, worms, trojans, and spyware. It infects devices to steal data, corrupt files, or give attackers remote control. Never install apps from unknown sources.

cards.title: Phishing
cards.body: Phishing tricks users into revealing credentials via fake emails, SMS (smishing), or voice calls (vishing). Red flags: urgency, mismatched sender domains, suspicious links, requests for OTPs.

cards.title: Ransomware
cards.body: Ransomware encrypts your files and demands payment to restore access. Notable examples: WannaCry (2017) affected 2 lakh+ systems globally. Regular offline backups are the best defence.

quiz.question: An email asks you to click a link immediately or your bank account will be suspended. This is most likely a:
quiz.explanation: Urgency + fake threat + suspicious link = classic phishing. Legitimate banks never ask for credentials via email.

cards.title: What Makes a Password Strong?
cards.body:
A strong password has:
• Minimum 12 characters
• Mix of uppercase, lowercase, numbers, symbols
• No dictionary words or personal info
• Unique for every account

Example: Tr0ub4dor&3 is stronger than password123

cards.title: Password Managers
cards.body: A password manager stores and auto-fills strong, unique passwords for every site. Recommended: Bitwarden (free, open-source), 1Password. You only remember one master password — the manager handles the rest.

cards.title: Two-Factor Authentication (2FA)
cards.body: 2FA adds a second layer beyond your password — typically an OTP sent to your phone or generated by an authenticator app. Enable 2FA on all critical accounts: banking, email, social media.

quiz.question: Which password is the strongest?
quiz.explanation: kX9#mQ2&vL!p is 12 characters with mixed case, numbers, and symbols — with no dictionary words or personal info. The others contain predictable patterns.

title: Safe Internet Practices
description: Browsing, social media, and Wi-Fi safety

cards.title: HTTPS vs HTTP
cards.body: Always look for HTTPS (the padlock icon) in the browser address bar. HTTPS encrypts data between your browser and the server. HTTP sends data in plain text — anyone on the same network can read it.

cards.title: Public Wi-Fi Risks
cards.body: Public Wi-Fi (cafes, airports, colleges) is unencrypted. Attackers can perform Man-in-the-Middle attacks to intercept your traffic. Avoid banking on public Wi-Fi. Use a VPN if you must.

cards.title: Social Engineering
cards.body: Social engineering manipulates people — not machines — into revealing information. Attackers impersonate colleagues, IT support, or authority figures. Rule: Never share OTPs or passwords with anyone, ever.

quiz.question: You're at a café and need to check your net banking. What should you do?
options: Use the café's free Wi-Fi — it's convenient
Use mobile data or a trusted VPN
Ask the café staff for the Wi-Fi password
It's fine as long as the site has HTTPS
quiz.explanation: Public Wi-Fi can be intercepted even on HTTPS sites via SSL stripping. Mobile data or a trusted VPN ensures an encrypted, private channel.

title: Advanced Threats & Defence
description: Network attacks, forensics and incident response

cards.title: DDoS Attacks
cards.body: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS) floods a server with traffic from thousands of compromised machines (a botnet), making it unavailable to legitimate users. Mitigation: rate limiting, CDN, WAF.

cards.title: SQL Injection
cards.body: SQL injection inserts malicious SQL code into input fields to manipulate a database. Example: entering ' OR '1'='1 into a login form might bypass authentication. Defence: parameterised queries, input validation.

cards.title: Zero-Day Exploits
cards.body: A zero-day is a vulnerability unknown to the software vendor — so there is zero days of protection. Attackers exploit it before a patch exists. Defence: network segmentation, behaviour-based detection.

quiz.question: What type of attack overwhelms a server by flooding it with traffic from multiple sources?
quiz.explanation: DDoS (Distributed Denial of Service) uses a botnet of compromised machines to flood the target, exhausting its resources and denying service to legitimate users.
